title: Meiji Restoration
summary: On 3 January 1868 a coup by samurai from the southwestern domains of Satsuma, Choshu and Tosa seized the imperial palace at Kyoto and issued the Osei Fukko decree, restoring direct rule to the fifteen-year-old Emperor Meiji and abolishing the shogunate that the Tokugawa family had held for two hundred and sixty-five years. Within a month the Boshin War had broken out; within eighteen months the last Tokugawa loyalists had surrendered at Hakodate. From this coup emerged the most rapid programme of state-directed modernisation in modern history and, within a generation, a Japan that could defeat China and then Russia in war.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Tokugawa shogunate had ruled Japan from Edo since 1603 under a system of controlled feudalism. The country was divided into some two hundred and sixty domains, each ruled by a hereditary daimyo whose income was measured in koku of rice; the shogun kept the peace among them through the sankin kotai system that required each lord to reside alternately in Edo and to leave his family there as hostage. The emperor and his court remained in Kyoto, cash-poor and ceremonial. Christianity had been suppressed since the 1630s; Europeans excluded except for a handful of Dutch merchants at the artificial island of Dejima in Nagasaki harbour; ordinary Japanese forbidden to leave the country on pain of death.

The system began to buckle when Commodore Matthew Perry's four black-hulled steam-and-sail warships anchored in Uraga Bay on 8 July 1853 and delivered a letter from the President of the United States demanding coaling stations for the Pacific whaling and China trade. Perry returned in February 1854 with a larger squadron; the shogunate, unable to expel him, signed the Convention of Kanagawa on 31 March 1854 opening two ports to American ships. The Harris Treaty of 29 July 1858, negotiated by the shogun's chief senior councillor Ii Naosuke without imperial approval, extended the concessions dramatically: five treaty ports, extraterritoriality, fixed low tariffs and free residence for foreign merchants in Yokohama, Nagasaki, Hakodate, Kobe and Niigata. Similar treaties with the Netherlands, Russia, Britain and France followed within weeks. The court in Kyoto, which had refused its sanction, became a rallying point for opponents of the shogunate.

The opposition organised around the slogan sonno joi, revere the emperor and expel the barbarian. Ii Naosuke's response, the Ansei Purge of 1858 to 1859, executed or exiled dozens of imperial-loyalist samurai; Ii himself was cut down by Mito ronin outside Edo Castle on 24 March 1860. The two most powerful outer domains, Satsuma in the south of Kyushu under the Shimazu family and Choshu at the western tip of Honshu under the Mori family, had never fully reconciled themselves to Tokugawa dominance since their ancestors' defeat at Sekigahara in 1600. Both suffered direct Western reprisals in the early 1860s: the British Royal Navy bombarded Kagoshima on 15 August 1863 in response to the killing of an English merchant at Namamugi, and an allied British, French, Dutch and American squadron destroyed the Choshu batteries at Shimonoseki in September 1864. These humiliations converted the young reformers of both domains, Saigo Takamori and Okubo Toshimichi of Satsuma, Kido Takayoshi and Ito Hirobumi of Choshu, from anti-foreign zealots into advocates of rapid Westernisation on their own terms. In March 1866 the swordsman Sakamoto Ryoma of Tosa brokered the secret Satcho Alliance uniting the two domains against the shogunate. The second Choshu expedition launched by the shogun that summer ended in humiliating failure. The shogun Tokugawa Iemochi died on 29 August 1866; his cousin Tokugawa Yoshinobu succeeded reluctantly. On 19 November 1867, in the face of the Satsuma-Choshu military build-up, Yoshinobu formally offered to return political authority to the emperor, a gesture called the taisei hokan meant to preserve his family's leading position in the reformed state.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Satsuma-Choshu leadership was not satisfied with a formal restoration that would leave Yoshinobu with his enormous domains and effective power. In the last days of December 1867 troops from Satsuma, Choshu, Tosa, Aki and Owari moved into Kyoto. The reforming courtier Iwakura Tomomi, working with Okubo and Saigo, drafted an imperial rescript. Before dawn on 3 January 1868, samurai of the allied domains seized the gates of the Kyoto Imperial Palace. Later the same day the young Emperor Meiji, born Mutsuhito on 3 November 1852 and only fifteen years old, publicly proclaimed the Osei Fukko, the Restoration of Imperial Rule. The Tokugawa shogunate was abolished; a new government of imperial princes, court nobles and samurai from the allied domains was constituted; and Yoshinobu was ordered to surrender his lands and titles. In the assembly that followed, Saigo Takamori was reported to have threatened his opponents with the sword; the confiscation was ratified.

Yoshinobu, in Osaka Castle with fifteen thousand troops, refused. On 27 and 28 January 1868 his forces attempted to march on Kyoto and were defeated at the Battle of Toba-Fushimi at the southern approaches to the city by a Satsuma-Choshu force less than a third their size. Superior Western drill, breech-loading rifles supplied by British merchants and the raising of the imperial brocade banner over the loyalist ranks, which retroactively made the shogunal forces enemies of the emperor, proved decisive. Yoshinobu fled by American warship from Osaka to Edo on 30 January. Imperial forces under Prince Arisugawa marched east along the Tokaido road; General Katsu Kaishu negotiated the bloodless surrender of Edo Castle on 3 May 1868. Pockets of resistance held out through the summer in the northeastern Ouetsu Reppan Domei, a coalition of northern domains crushed at the fall of Wakamatsu on 6 November 1868. A rump Tokugawa naval force under Enomoto Takeaki sailed to Hokkaido and proclaimed the short-lived Republic of Ezo; it surrendered at Hakodate on 27 June 1869.

Even as the war was being fought the new government began the fundamental reconstruction of Japan. On 6 April 1868 the emperor promulgated the Charter Oath, a five-article statement drafted by Kido Takayoshi that promised deliberative assemblies, an end to social class barriers on employment, the freedom for commoners to pursue their calling, the abandonment of evil customs of the past, and the pursuit of knowledge throughout the world. Later that year the era name was changed to Meiji, meaning enlightened rule. The capital moved from Kyoto to Edo, which was renamed Tokyo, in October 1868. In August 1871 the domains were abolished and replaced with prefectures administered from Tokyo, ending eight hundred years of feudal decentralisation with a stroke of the pen. Compulsory primary education was introduced in 1872; a conscription army modelled on the French drew commoners into what had been a purely samurai profession; the Land Tax Reform of 1873 replaced feudal rice dues with a monetary tax paid by farmers; a new nationwide postal service, a national bank, a railway system, telegraph lines, shipyards, textile factories, silk filatures and the great state industries at Yokosuka and Osaka were built in the following decade. Foreign advisers, the oyatoi gaikokujin, were hired in the thousands, from British railway engineers and German legal scholars to French army officers and American schoolteachers; young samurai were sent abroad in equal numbers to study.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The transformation was not uncontested. The abolition of samurai stipends in 1876 and the ban on the wearing of swords by anyone but soldiers and police provoked scattered uprisings in the western provinces. The former Satsuma hero Saigo Takamori, disillusioned by the direction of the reforms, led a full-scale rebellion of some thirty thousand ex-samurai in 1877; it was crushed by the new conscript army at the siege of Kumamoto and the battle of Shiroyama on 24 September 1877, where Saigo committed seppuku. His defeat by the army he had helped create marked the definitive triumph of the modernising state over the samurai class.

In 1881 the government promised a constitution and a parliament by 1890. Ito Hirobumi, now the leading Choshu statesman, spent 1882 to 1883 in Europe studying constitutional models and settled on the Prussian precedent as most compatible with imperial authority. The Meiji Constitution, promulgated as a gift from the emperor on 11 February 1889, established a two-chamber Diet, guaranteed a limited catalogue of rights subject to law, and located sovereignty in the emperor as a sacred and inviolable person. It endured until 1947. Japan had become the first non-Western state with a modern constitution.

The economic transformation was equally rapid. Silk and tea exports funded the import of foreign machinery; the state-owned Tomioka silk mill of 1872, Japan's first modern factory, was one of many transferred to private ownership in the 1880s to underwrite the emerging zaibatsu conglomerates of Mitsui, Mitsubishi, Sumitomo and Yasuda. Railways connected Tokyo and Yokohama in 1872 and reached most major cities by 1900. By 1890 Japan was producing warships, guns and locomotives in its own arsenals. The great war for revision of the unequal treaties, waged in parallel through diplomacy and legal reform, culminated in the abolition of extraterritoriality in 1899 and full tariff autonomy in 1911.

The most spectacular external demonstration was military. In the First Sino-Japanese War of 1894 and 1895 Japan defeated the Qing empire, took Taiwan and Korea out of the Chinese sphere and joined the ranks of the imperial powers by acquiring its first colonies. In the Russo-Japanese War of 1904 and 1905 it defeated a European great power, sank two Russian fleets and won a treaty settlement that gave it Port Arthur, southern Sakhalin and predominant influence in Korea, which it annexed in 1910. To Asian nationalists from India to Egypt, Japan became evidence that Western predominance was not natural or permanent. To Western observers, it became a warning that the balance of world power might shift eastward within a single lifetime.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Meiji Restoration remains the classic case of a state-directed modernisation from above. Historians have long debated whether it was a revolution, a coup, a restoration or a bourgeois transformation in samurai dress. The men who made it, Saigo, Okubo, Kido, Ito, Iwakura and Yamagata Aritomo, called themselves loyalists restoring an ancient constitution; the historian E. H. Norman in the 1940s treated their work as an incomplete bourgeois revolution; more recent scholarship has emphasised the continuities with late Tokugawa reforms and the crucial role of foreign pressure. What is not disputed is the speed and reach of the transformation. Between 1868 and 1912, the year of Meiji's death, Japan went from a closed feudal society to a constitutional imperial state with a modern army and navy, universal education, a functioning parliament and a diversified industrial economy.

The personal cult of the emperor built up during the Meiji era proved enormously consequential. The Kyoto court that had been nearly invisible for eight centuries was reinvented as the sacred centre of national life; imperial portraits were distributed to every school and office, imperial birthdays became national holidays, the Yasukuni Shrine, established in 1869, enshrined the war dead as national deities. This state Shinto framework survived the Meiji constitution and shaped Japanese political culture until the Allied Occupation of 1945 to 1952 disestablished it.

Abroad, the Meiji example inspired imitation from the Ottoman Young Turks to the reformers of late Qing China, from Egyptian modernisers to Thai administrators. The direct product of the Restoration, however, was not always benign. The industrial and military capacity built up in the Meiji era enabled the imperialism of the 1930s and 1940s that ended in the catastrophe of the Pacific War. The reform of Japanese society in the postwar constitution of 1947 was often framed as a completion of the democratic promise of the 1868 Charter Oath, an attempt to fulfil the deliberative and egalitarian side of Meiji that the constitutional monarchy had partially suppressed. Today the Meiji-jingu shrine in Tokyo, dedicated to the emperor and empress in 1920, remains one of Japan's most visited sites, a reminder that the coup of 3 January 1868 is celebrated as the founding moment of modern Japan.

worldContext.text: The United States was in the second year of Reconstruction under President Andrew Johnson, who narrowly survived impeachment in the Senate on 26 May 1868. On 28 July 1868 the Fourteenth Amendment guaranteeing citizenship and equal protection was proclaimed ratified. In Canada, the first prime minister Sir John A. Macdonald was consolidating the Confederation that had come into being on 1 July 1867. In Latin America, the War of the Triple Alliance between Paraguay and the combined forces of Brazil, Argentina and Uruguay was in its final devastating phase.

The German unification project was accelerating under Otto von Bismarck; the North German Confederation had been formed after the Austro-Prussian War of 1866, and the Franco-Prussian War that would produce the German Empire lay only three years ahead. In Britain the second Reform Act of 1867 had roughly doubled the electorate. In Spain the Glorious Revolution of September 1868 overthrew Queen Isabella II. The First International, the International Workingmen's Association founded in London in 1864 with Karl Marx as its most active member, held its third congress in Brussels in September 1868.

British India was under the viceroyalty of Sir John Lawrence and, from January 1869, Lord Mayo. The Sirhind Canal system and the network of trunk railways were being extended; the Punjab and North-West Frontier were being consolidated. The Second Anglo-Afghan War lay ahead in 1878. In the princely states, Ranjit Singh's Punjab empire had long been absorbed but the Kashmiri, Hyderabad, Baroda and Mysore states remained under indirect rule.

The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Abdulaziz was still in the Tanzimat reforming period, though the sultan's foreign debts and lavish palace-building were beginning the fiscal crisis that would erupt in 1875 in default. Egypt under Isma'il Pasha was pushing the Suez Canal, opened in November 1869, toward completion. In Ethiopia the emperor Tewodros II died at the storming of his mountain fortress of Magdala by a British expeditionary force on 13 April 1868 in an early example of a European punitive expedition into the African interior.

In West Africa the Sokoto Caliphate continued to dominate the Hausa and Fulani lands. The discovery of the first great diamonds on the Vaal River in 1867 was drawing prospectors into southern Africa and would within a decade transform the sleepy Cape Colony and the Boer republics into contested economic prizes. King Cetshwayo consolidated power among the Zulu; Basotholand became a British protectorate in March 1868 under King Moshoeshoe I to escape Boer encroachment. Portuguese, French, British and German traders operated coastal enclaves from Cape Verde to Zanzibar.

In New Zealand the Land Wars between the settler government and various Maori communities continued. The prophet-general Titokowaru led a Maori campaign in south Taranaki that inflicted a series of defeats on the colonial forces in 1868. In eastern Australia, transportation of British convicts to Western Australia ended on 10 January 1868 with the arrival of the last convict ship, the Hougoumont. The Australian colonies were increasingly self-governing and were beginning the conversations that would produce federation in 1901.

figures.name: Emperor Meiji
figures.role: Fifteen-year-old sovereign in whose name the Restoration was proclaimed; his forty-five-year reign gave its name to the era

figures.name: Tokugawa Yoshinobu
figures.role: Fifteenth and last shogun; offered to return power in November 1867 and was militarily deposed at Toba-Fushimi

figures.name: Saigo Takamori
figures.role: Satsuma samurai and military leader of the imperial forces; later led the failed Satsuma Rebellion of 1877 and became a national tragic hero

figures.name: Okubo Toshimichi
figures.role: Satsuma statesman and one of the principal architects of the Meiji government's centralising reforms until his assassination in 1878

figures.name: Kido Takayoshi
figures.role: Choshu leader and drafter of the Charter Oath of 1868

figures.name: Iwakura Tomomi
figures.role: Court noble who choreographed the Osei Fukko decree and led the great embassy that toured Europe and America in 1871 to 1873

figures.name: Ito Hirobumi
figures.role: Choshu samurai turned statesman; author of the Meiji Constitution and the first prime minister of Japan

images.caption: The Meiji Emperor in military dress, albumen silver print by Uchida Kuichi, 1873. Cleveland Museum of Art.
images.credit: Uchida Kuichi (1844 to 1875), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Conte portrait of the Emperor Meiji by Edoardo Chiossone, produced from covert sketches at the Imperial Printing Bureau.
